parts.content: Calculate the electric potential at a point P located $0.40\ \text{m}$ from $q_1$ and $0.30\ \text{m}$ from $q_2$.
parts.markscheme:
$V_1 = \frac{kq_1}{r_1} = \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.40} = 1.35 \times 10^5\ \text{V}$

$V_2 = \frac{kq_2}{r_2} = \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times (-2.0) \times 10^{-6}}{0.30} = -5.99 \times 10^4\ \text{V}$

$V_P = V_1 + V_2 = 1.35 \times 10^5 - 5.99 \times 10^4 = 7.49 \times 10^4\ \text{V}$

Correct $V_1$ and $V_2$ <marks>A1</marks>

Correct final result <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the potential formula
steps.content:
To find the electric potential at point P, we use the principle of superposition. This means we calculate the individual potential contribution from each charge using the formula for point charge potential and then sum them algebraically. 

Electric potential $V$ is a scalar quantity, so we do not need to worry about directions, but we must include the signs of the charges.

steps.title: Calculate potential from q1
steps.content:
Using the formula $$V = \frac{kq}{r}$$, we calculate the potential $V_1$ due to charge $q_1$ ($+6.0\ \mu\text{C}$) at a distance of $0.40\ \text{m}$.

$$\begin{aligned} V_1 &= \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.40} \\ V_1 &= 1.3485 \times 10^5 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to appropriate significant figures, $V_1 \approx 1.35 \times 10^5 \text{ V}$.

calculator.description: Calculate V1

steps.title: Calculate potential from q2
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the potential $V_2$ due to charge $q_2$ ($-2.0\ \mu\text{C}$) at a distance of $0.30\ \text{m}$. Note that since $q_2$ is negative, the potential is also negative.

$$\begin{aligned} V_2 &= \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times (-2.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.30} \\ V_2 &\approx -5.9933 \times 10^4 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to appropriate significant figures, $V_2 \approx -5.99 \times 10^4 \text{ V}$.

calculator.description: Calculate V2

steps.title: Sum the potentials
steps.content:
Finally, we sum the individual potentials to find the total electric potential at point P ($V_P$):

$$\begin{aligned} V_P &= V_1 + V_2 \\ V_P &= 1.3485 \times 10^5 + (-5.9933 \times 10^4) \\ V_P &= 7.4917 \times 10^4 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

Rounded to three significant figures, the result is $7.49 \times 10^4 \text{ V}$.

Marks are awarded for finding both $V_1$ and $V_2$ correctly (A1) and then finding the correct algebraic sum (A1).

calculator.description: Sum potentials

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To calculate the total electric potential at point P, we first need to identify the charges and their respective distances from that point. 

From the question, we have:
- Charge 1: $q_1 = +6.0\ \mu\text{C} = 6.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$ at $r_1 = 0.40\ \text{m}$
- Charge 2: $q_2 = -2.0\ \mu\text{C} = -2.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$ at $r_2 = 0.30\ \text{m}$
- Coulomb constant: $k = 8.99 \times 10^9\ \text{N m}^2 \text{C}^{-2}$

steps.title: Formulate combined expression
steps.content:
Electric potential is a scalar quantity, meaning the total potential at a point is simply the algebraic sum of the potentials from each individual charge. The potential $V$ of a point charge is given by $V = \frac{kq}{r}$.

By applying the principle of superposition, we can combine these into a single algebraic expression:

$$V_P = V_1 + V_2 = \frac{kq_1}{r_1} + \frac{kq_2}{r_2}$$

Factoring out $k$ gives us our calculation template:

$$V_P = k \left( \frac{q_1}{r_1} + \frac{q_2}{r_2} \right)$$ 

This approach is covered in **Topic D.2: Electric and magnetic fields**.

steps.title: Calculate using GDC
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values into the single expression. Remember that for electric potential, the **sign of the charge matters**. A negative charge will create a negative potential.

$$V_P = 8.99 \times 10^9 \times \left( \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.40} + \frac{-2.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.30} \right)$$

Using a calculator for this single-line calculation ensures accuracy and saves time during the exam.

gdc.instructions: Enter the combined algebraic expression directly into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the following expression on the home screen: 8.99E9 * ((6E-6 / 0.40) + (-2E-6 / 0.30))

desmos.instructions: Type the equation into the expression bar to find the total potential.

calculator.description: Calculate the total electric potential at point P using the combined formula.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The result from the calculation is approximately $74916.67\ \text{V}$. 

Following IB conventions for significant figures (rounding to 3 sig figs based on the given data), the final result is:

$$V_P = 7.49 \times 10^4\ \text{V}$$

In the markscheme, you earn **1 mark** for correctly identifying the individual potentials (or showing them in the combined sum) and **1 mark** for the correct final answer with units.

parts.content: Determine the work done to bring a $+1.0\ \mu\text{C}$ charge from infinity to point P.
parts.markscheme:
$W = qV = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 7.49 \times 10^4 = 0.0749\ \text{J}$

Correct formula/substitution <marks>M1</marks>

Correct final result <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the potential method
steps.content:
To find the work done to bring a charge from infinity to a specific point, we use the concept of **Electric Potential ($V$)**. The work done ($W$) is given by the product of the moved charge ($q$) and the potential at that point ($V$):

$$W = qV$$

Since potential at infinity is defined as zero, the work done is simply the energy required to place the charge at point P. This is covered in **Topic D.2 Electric and magnetic fields**.

steps.title: Determine distances to Point P
steps.content:
To calculate the potential at point P, we need the distances from each source charge to P. Based on the configuration, point P is located at a distance of $r_1 = 0.40\text{ m}$ from $q_1$ and $r_2 = 0.30\text{ m}$ from $q_2$. 

Note that these distances satisfy the Pythagorean theorem with the $0.50\text{ m}$ separation between the charges ($0.3^2 + 0.4^2 = 0.5^2$), indicating P forms a right-angled triangle with the charges.

highlights.text: separated by a distance of 0.50 m

steps.title: Calculate individual electric potentials
steps.content:
We use the formula for the electric potential of a point charge:

$$V = \frac{kq}{r}$$

Using the constant $k = 8.99 \times 10^9\text{ N m}^2\text{C}^{-2}$ from the data booklet:

**For $q_1$ ($+6.0\ μ\text{C}$):**
$$V_1 = \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.40} = 134,850\text{ V}$$

**For $q_2$ ($-2.0\ μ\text{C}$):**
$$V_2 = \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times -2.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.30} \approx -59,933\text{ V}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the two terms individually or sum them in one go.

desmos.instructions: Input the potential sum formula.

calculator.description: Calculate potentials V1 and V2.

steps.title: Sum the total potential
steps.content:
Electric potential is a scalar quantity, so we simply add the values together to find the total potential at P ($V_P$):

$$\begin{aligned} V_P &= V_1 + V_2 \\ V_P &= 134,850 + (-59,933) \\ V_P &\approx 74,917\text{ V} \approx 7.49 \times 10^4\text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the work done
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the work done ($W$) to bring the test charge $q_{test} = +1.0\ μ\text{C}$ from infinity to point P:

$$\begin{aligned} W &= q_{test} \times V_P \\ W &= (1.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}) \times (7.49 \times 10^4\text{ V}) \\ W &= 0.0749\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the expected value of **$0.0749\text{ J}$**.

gdc.instructions: Multiply the charge by the result for Vp.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the charge by the previously calculated potential: 1.0E-6 * 74917.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the charge by the potential result.

calculator.description: Calculate final work W.

steps.title: Relate Work to Potential Energy
steps.content:
To find the work done to bring a charge from infinity to a specific point, we use the principle that work done ($W$) is equal to the change in electric potential energy ($\Delta E_p$). Since the potential energy at infinity is defined as zero, the work done is simply the total electric potential energy of the system at point $P$.

This concept is covered in **Topic D.2: Electric and magnetic fields** (specifically section D.2.4).

highlights.text: work done to bring a $+1.0\ \mu\text{C}$ charge from infinity to point P

steps.title: Calculate Individual Potential Energies
steps.content:
The total work done is the sum of the electric potential energies between the incoming test charge ($q_3 = +1.0\ \mu\text{C}$) and each of the source charges already in place ($q_1$ and $q_2$). We use the formula for electric potential energy:

$$E_p = \frac{k q_1 q_2}{r}$$

Where:
- $k = 8.99 \times 10^9\ \text{N m}^2\ \text{C}^{-2}$ (Coulomb's constant)
- $q_1, q_2$ are the magnitudes of the charges (including signs)
- $r$ is the distance between them

steps.title: Sum the Energies
steps.content:
The total work done ($W$) is the sum of the potential energy due to $q_1$ and the potential energy due to $q_2$:

$$W = \frac{k q_1 q_3}{r_1} + \frac{k q_2 q_3}{r_2}$$

From the previous part of the question (calculating electric potential $V$ at $P$), we know that the sum of the terms $\left( \frac{k q_1}{r_1} + \frac{k q_2}{r_2} \right)$ resulted in a potential $V = 7.49 \times 10^4\ \text{V}$. 

Substituting our test charge $q_3 = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$:

$$W = q_3 \times V = (1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}) \times (7.49 \times 10^4\ \text{V})$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the multiplication of the charge (1E-6) and the potential (7.49E4).

ti84.instructions: Multiply the charge value by the potential value.

desmos.instructions: Input the multiplication of the test charge and the electric potential at point P.

calculator.description: Calculate the final work done by multiplying the charge and the total potential.

steps.title: State Final Result
steps.content:
Performing the calculation:

$$W = 0.0749\ \text{J}$$

In the IB markscheme, you earn **M1** for showing the correct substitution of values into the work formula and **A1** for the correct final answer with units.

parts.content: Calculate the electric field strength at point P due to each charge and determine the net electric field vector relation at point P.
parts.markscheme:
$E_1 = \frac{k|q_1|}{r_1^2} = \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.40^2} = 3.37 \times 10^5\ \text{N C}^{-1}$

$E_2 = \frac{k|q_2|}{r_2^2} = \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.30^2} = 2.00 \times 10^5\ \text{N C}^{-1}$

Since $0.30^2 + 0.40^2 = 0.50^2$, the geometry is a right-angled triangle. $E_1$ and $E_2$ are orthogonal.

$E_1$ and $E_2$ calculated correctly <marks>A1</marks>

Clear vector relation (Pythagoras) <marks>M1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the field formula
steps.content:
To calculate the electric field strength ($E$) at point P due to a point charge, we use the formula from the data booklet:

$$E = \frac{k|q|}{r^2}$$

where $k$ is Coulomb's constant, $q$ is the magnitude of the charge, and $r$ is the distance from the charge to point P.

steps.title: Calculate magnitude of E1
steps.content:
First, we calculate the field strength produced by $q_1 = +6.0\ \mu\text{C}$ at a distance of $r_1 = 0.40\ \text{m}$:

$$\begin{aligned} E_1 &= \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (6.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.40^2} \\ E_1 &= 3.37 \times 10^5\ \text{N C}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This field points away from $q_1$ because it is a positive charge.

gdc.instructions: Enter (8.99 * 10^9 * 6.0 * 10^-6) / (0.40^2) into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter (8.99e9 * 6.0e-6) / 0.40^2 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (8.99 * 10^9 * 6.0 * 10^-6) / 0.40^2 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculation of E1 magnitude

steps.title: Calculate magnitude of E2
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the field strength produced by $q_2 = -2.0\ \mu\text{C}$ at a distance of $r_2 = 0.30\ \text{m}$:

$$\begin{aligned} E_2 &= \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.30^2} \\ E_2 &= 2.00 \times 10^5\ \text{N C}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Note that we use the absolute value of the charge. This field points toward $q_2$ because it is a negative charge.

gdc.instructions: Enter (8.99 * 10^9 * 2.0 * 10^-6) / (0.30^2) into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter (8.99e9 * 2.0e-6) / 0.30^2 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (8.99 * 10^9 * 2.0 * 10^-6) / 0.30^2 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculation of E2 magnitude

steps.title: Verify geometric orthogonality
steps.content:
To determine the vector relation, we check the geometry of the triangle formed by $q_1$, $q_2$, and $P$. We are given the side lengths $0.30\ \text{m}$, $0.40\ \text{m}$, and the separation $0.50\ \text{m}$.

Using the Pythagorean theorem converse:
$$0.30^2 + 0.40^2 = 0.09 + 0.16 = 0.25$$
$$0.50^2 = 0.25$$

Since $r_1^2 + r_2^2 = d^2$, the triangle is right-angled at point P. This means the electric field vectors $E_1$ and $E_2$ are perpendicular (orthogonal) to each other.

steps.title: Determine net vector relation
steps.content:
Since $E_1$ and $E_2$ are orthogonal, the magnitude of the net electric field ($E_{net}$) is found using the Pythagorean theorem for vectors:

$$E_{net} = \sqrt{E_1^2 + E_2^2}$$

or simply the relation:
$$E_{net}^2 = E_1^2 + E_2^2$$

This confirms that the net field is the vector sum of two perpendicular components.

steps.title: Analyze the Geometry
steps.content:
Before calculating, we need to understand the spatial arrangement of the charges and point P. The distances given are $0.40\text{ m}$ from $q_1$ and $0.30\text{ m}$ from $q_2$, with the charges themselves separated by $0.50\text{ m}$. 

We can check if this forms a right-angled triangle using the Pythagorean theorem:
$$0.30^2 + 0.40^2 = 0.09 + 0.16 = 0.25$$
Since $0.50^2 = 0.25$, the vectors $\vec{E}_1$ and $\vec{E}_2$ at point P are perpendicular (orthogonal) to each other. This geometry is covered in **Topic D.2: Electric and magnetic fields**.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the sum of the squares of 0.30 and 0.40.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the squares of the shorter sides.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expression to see if it equals 0.25 (0.50 squared).

calculator.description: Verify the Pythagorean relationship of the distances.

steps.title: Calculate Field from $q_1$
steps.content:
Using the electric field formula from the data booklet, we calculate the magnitude of the field produced by $q_1$ at point P. Since $q_1$ is positive, this field vector points away from the charge.

$$E = k\frac{q}{r^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} E_1 &= \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (6.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.40^2} \\ E_1 &= 3.37 \times 10^5\text{ N C}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Input the constants and variables into the fraction template.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation using scientific notation (EE key).

desmos.instructions: Calculate the result for E1.

calculator.description: Calculate the electric field magnitude for the first charge.

steps.title: Calculate Field from $q_2$
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the magnitude of the field produced by $q_2$. Since $q_2$ is negative, this field vector points toward the charge.

$$\begin{aligned} E_2 &= \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.30^2} \\ E_2 &= 2.00 \times 10^5\text{ N C}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate E2 using the field formula constants.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the field strength for the 2.0 microCoulomb charge.

calculator.description: Calculate the electric field magnitude for the second charge.

steps.title: Determine Net Field Relation
steps.content:
To find the net field using the **Vector Component Method**, we align our axes with the individual fields. 

Since we proved in Step 0 that the fields are perpendicular, we can treat $E_1$ as the $y$-component and $E_2$ as the $x$-component. The relationship between these vector components and the net electric field magnitude ($E_{net}$) is given by the Pythagorean theorem:

$$E_{net} = \sqrt{E_1^2 + E_2^2}$$

This completes the requirements of the question: we have the individual strengths and the vector relationship that defines the net field.

parts.content: Find the magnitude of the net electric field at point P.
parts.markscheme:
$E_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{(3.37 \times 10^5)^2 + (2.00 \times 10^5)^2} = 3.92 \times 10^5\ \text{N C}^{-1}$

<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Recall the net field principle
steps.content: To find the magnitude of the net electric field at point P, we need to consider the electric fields produced by $q_1$ and $q_2$ as vectors. Since we are using analytical vector addition, we treat the individual field magnitudes as perpendicular components (based on the geometry of the problem) and combine them using the Pythagorean theorem.

steps.title: Identify individual field magnitudes
steps.content:
From previous parts of the problem (or by using the electric field formula $$E=k\frac{q}{r^{2}}$$ from the data booklet), the magnitudes of the electric fields at point P are:

- Field from $q_1$: $E_1 = 3.37 \times 10^5 \text{ N C}^{-1}$
- Field from $q_2$: $E_2 = 2.00 \times 10^5 \text{ N C}^{-1}$

steps.title: Apply the Pythagorean theorem
steps.content:
Because these two field vectors are perpendicular at point P, we can find the magnitude of the resultant (net) field, $E_{\text{net}}$, using the Pythagorean theorem:

$$E_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{E_1^2 + E_2^2}$$

Substituting our values:

$$E_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{(3.37 \times 10^5)^2 + (2.00 \times 10^5)^2}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final magnitude
steps.content:
Using a calculator to solve for the magnitude, we get:

$$E_{\text{net}} \approx 3.92 \times 10^5 \text{ N C}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the square root function and carefully enter the scientific notation values.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen using the square root and squared keys.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of the net electric field.

parts.content: Explain why the direction of the electric field is not the same as the direction of the electric potential gradient in this case.
parts.markscheme:
The electric field is defined as the negative gradient of the electric potential ($−\nabla V$) <marks>A1</marks>

This means the field points in the direction where the potential decreases most rapidly, opposite to the gradient <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Define the relationship
steps.content:
To understand why the electric field and the potential gradient have different directions, we look at the mathematical relationship between them. This concept is covered in **Topic D.2: Electric and magnetic fields**. 

The electric field $E$ is defined as the negative gradient of the electric potential $V$. We express this relationship as:

$$E = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}$$

In more advanced notation, this is written as $E = -\nabla V$.

steps.title: Understand the gradient direction
steps.content:
In physics and mathematics, the **gradient** of a scalar field (like electric potential) is a vector that points in the direction of the steepest **increase** of that value. 

So, if you were to follow the direction of the potential gradient, you would be moving toward areas of higher electric potential (closer to the positive charge $q_1$).

steps.title: Analyze the negative sign
steps.content:
The formula $$E = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}$$ includes a crucial **negative sign**. 

This sign tells us that the electric field vector $E$ points in the direction where the potential **decreases** most rapidly. While the gradient points "uphill" (toward higher potential), the electric field points "downhill" (toward lower potential).

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Because the electric field is the negative of the potential gradient, their directions must be exactly opposite. 

In this system, the potential decreases as you move away from the positive charge $q_1$ and toward the negative charge $q_2$. Therefore, the electric field points from $q_1$ to $q_2$, which is the opposite direction of the gradient.

highlights.text: The electric field is defined as the negative gradient of the electric potential ($−\nabla V$)
This means the field points in the direction where the potential decreases most rapidly, opposite to the gradient

steps.title: Define the potential gradient
steps.content: The electric potential gradient is a vector that points in the direction of the greatest **increase** in electric potential ($V$). Think of it like looking uphill on a topographical map; the gradient points straight up the steepest part of the hill.

steps.title: Analyze the electric field
steps.content: The electric field ($E$) represents the force exerted on a positive test charge. In terms of work and energy, a positive charge naturally moves from a region of high potential to a region of low potential to minimize its potential energy. Therefore, the electric field always points in the direction of the greatest **decrease** in potential.

steps.title: Compare the directions
steps.content:
Since the potential gradient points "uphill" (towards higher potential) and the electric field points "downhill" (towards lower potential), they are in opposite directions. Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$E = -\nabla V$$

In this specific case with $q_1$ and $q_2$, the field lines directed from the positive charge to the negative charge show the path a positive charge would take as it loses potential energy, which is exactly opposite to the direction of increasing potential.

steps.title: Understand electric field lines
steps.content:
To understand the direction of the electric field, we look at field lines. In this setup, we have a positive charge $q_1$ and a negative charge $q_2$. 

Electric field lines always originate from positive charges and terminate on negative charges. This means the electric field points from regions of **higher electric potential** to regions of **lower electric potential**.

steps.title: Define the potential gradient
steps.content:
In mathematics and physics, a **gradient** vector represents the direction and magnitude of the steepest increase of a scalar quantity. 

For electric potential ($V$), the potential gradient points in the direction where the potential increases most rapidly. In our case, this vector would point toward the positive charge ($q_1$), where the potential is higher.

steps.title: Compare the directions
steps.content:
Now we can see the conflict in directions:
1. The **electric field** points in the direction of decreasing potential (from $q_1$ to $q_2$).
2. The **potential gradient** points in the direction of increasing potential (towards $q_1$).

Because they point in exactly opposite directions, we say the electric field is the **negative gradient** of the electric potential.

highlights.text: direction of the electric field is not the same as the direction of the electric potential gradient

steps.title: Relate to the formula
steps.content:
This relationship is expressed by the formula:

$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V$$

In the IB curriculum (Topic D.2.4), you might recognize this conceptually as the field being the rate of change of potential with distance, but with a negative sign to show it points "downhill."

- The negative sign indicates that the field points **opposite** to the direction of maximum increase (the gradient).
- This ensures that a positive test charge placed in the field will naturally move toward lower potential.

parts.content: State the direction of the electric field between the plates.
parts.markscheme: The electric field is directed from the positive plate to the negative plate / downward <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify plate polarity
steps.content:
From the question description and diagram, we can identify the charge on each plate:
- The **upper plate** is positively charged.
- The **lower plate** is negatively charged.

steps.title: Apply field convention
steps.content:
By definition, the direction of an electric field ($E$) is the direction of the force that would be exerted on a positive test charge. 

Following the **Standard Field Convention**, electric field lines:
1. Originate from **positive** charges/plates.
2. Terminate on **negative** charges/plates.

steps.title: Determine the direction
steps.content: Since the field lines must point from the positive plate to the negative plate, the electric field between these plates is directed **downwards** (or from the upper plate to the lower plate).

highlights.text: The electric field is directed from the positive plate to the negative plate / downward

steps.title: Define electric field direction
steps.content:
To find the direction of an electric field, we look at the direction of the force that would act on a positive test charge placed in that field. 

In the **Fields** section of your data booklet, the relationship between force and electric field is given by:

$$E = \frac{F}{q}$$

This formula tells us that for a positive charge ($q > 0$), the electric field vector $\vec{E}$ and the force vector $\vec{F}$ point in the same direction.

steps.title: Analyze the plate charges
steps.content: The question specifies the arrangement of the charges on the plates. We know that the upper plate has a positive charge and the lower plate has a negative charge.

steps.title: Determine force on proton
steps.content:
Let's consider the proton shown in the diagram. Since a proton is a positive charge:
1. It will be **repelled** by the positive upper plate.
2. It will be **attracted** toward the negative lower plate.

Both of these interactions result in a net force acting **downwards** on the proton.

steps.title: State the final direction
steps.content:
Since the force on a positive charge (the proton) is directed downwards, the electric field must also be directed **downwards**.

By convention, electric field lines always point away from positive charges and toward negative charges.

steps.title: Identify charge and potential
steps.content:
To determine the direction using potential gradient analysis, we first look at the charges on the plates. The question specifies that the **upper plate is positively charged** and the **lower plate is negatively charged**.

In electrostatics, we associate positive charges with a higher electric potential ($V$) and negative charges with a lower electric potential.

steps.title: Apply potential gradient rule
steps.content:
The electric field direction is fundamentally linked to the "gradient" (or change) of the electric potential. By definition, electric field lines always point away from regions of higher potential and toward regions of lower potential.

This is a core property covered in **Topic D.2: Electric and magnetic fields**.

steps.title: Determine the field direction
steps.content:
Since the field must point from the higher potential (positive plate) to the lower potential (negative plate), and the positive plate is on top, the field must point vertically down.

Therefore, the direction of the electric field is **downward**.

parts.content: State the direction of the electric force on the proton and the electron.
parts.markscheme:
Proton: force is downward (same direction as field) <marks>A1</marks>

Electron: force is upward (opposite to field) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify plate and particle charges
steps.content:
To determine the direction of the force using electrostatic attraction and repulsion, we first identify the charges involved:

*   **Upper plate:** Positive ($+$)
*   **Lower plate:** Negative ($-$)
*   **Proton:** Positive charge ($+$)
*   **Electron:** Negative charge ($-$)

Recall the fundamental law of electrostatics: like charges repel each other, and opposite charges attract each other.

steps.title: Analyze force on the proton
steps.content:
Since the proton is positively charged:

1. It is **repelled** by the positive upper plate.
2. It is **attracted** to the negative lower plate.

Both of these interactions act in the same direction, pushing the proton away from the top and pulling it toward the bottom. Therefore, the electric force on the proton is **downward**.

steps.title: Analyze force on the electron
steps.content:
Since the electron is negatively charged:

1. It is **repelled** by the negative lower plate.
2. It is **attracted** to the positive upper plate.

In this case, the repulsion from the bottom and the attraction to the top both act in the same direction. Therefore, the electric force on the electron is **upward**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on our analysis of electrostatic attraction and repulsion:

*   **Proton:** Downward
*   **Electron:** Upward

In an IB exam, you earn 1 mark for each correct direction. Note that the force on the proton is in the same direction as the electric field (which goes from positive to negative), while the force on the electron is opposite to the field.

steps.title: Determine electric field direction
steps.content:
First, let's identify the direction of the uniform electric field ($E$). By convention, electric field lines always point away from positive charges and toward negative charges.

Since the **upper plate is positively charged** and the **lower plate is negatively charged**, the electric field vector points vertically **downward**.

steps.title: Relate force to field
steps.content:
To find the direction of the force, we use the relationship between the electric field and the electric force from the data booklet:

$$E = \frac{F}{q} \implies F = qE$$

From this equation, we can determine the direction of the force based on the sign of the charge $q$:
- For a **positive charge**, the force is in the **same direction** as the electric field.
- For a **negative charge**, the force is in the **opposite direction** to the electric field.

steps.title: Apply to each particle
steps.content:
Now we apply these rules to the two particles:

1.  **Proton**: A proton has a positive charge ($+e$). Since it is positive, the force is in the same direction as the field. Therefore, the force on the proton is **downward**.
2.  **Electron**: An electron has a negative charge ($-e$). Since it is negative, the force is opposite to the direction of the field. Therefore, the force on the electron is **upward**.

parts.content: Compare the magnitudes of the electric force on the proton and the electron.
parts.markscheme:
The magnitudes of the electric force are equal <marks>A1</marks>

Since $F = qE$ and both particles have charge $q$ of the same magnitude.

steps.title: Analyze the electric field
steps.content: The question specifies that the **electric field is uniform** between the two parallel plates. This is a crucial piece of information because it means the electric field strength, $E$, is constant and identical at every point between the plates. Therefore, both the proton and the electron experience the same field strength.

steps.title: Identify the force formula
steps.content:
To find the force on a charged particle in an electric field, we look at the **Fields** section of the data booklet. The relationship between electric field strength $E$, force $F$, and charge $q$ is:

$$E = \frac{F}{q}$$

By rearranging this formula, we can express the magnitude of the electric force as:

$$F = qE$$

steps.title: Compare charge magnitudes
steps.content:
Next, we consider the charge of each particle. While they have opposite signs (proton is positive, electron is negative), their **magnitudes** are identical and equal to the elementary charge $e$.

$$\text{Magnitude of proton charge } |q_p| = e$$
$$\text{Magnitude of electron charge } |q_e| = e$$

According to the physical constants in your data booklet, this value is approximately $$1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$$.

steps.title: Conclude the comparison
steps.content:
Since both particles are in the same uniform electric field ($E$) and both have the same magnitude of charge ($e$), we can calculate the force magnitude for each:

$$\text{Force on proton: } F_p = e \times E$$
$$\text{Force on electron: } F_e = e \times E$$

Because the values for $e$ and $E$ are the same for both particles, the magnitudes of the electric force are equal.

parts.content: Compare the magnitudes of the acceleration of the proton and the electron.
parts.markscheme:
The electron has a smaller mass than the proton <marks>M1</marks>

Since $a = \frac{F}{m}$ and $F$ is the same for both, the electron has a greater acceleration than the proton <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the electric potential at the surface of the sphere.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the given values into the formula for electric potential: $V = \frac{kQ}{r} = \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.15}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final value: $V = 3.60 \times 10^5\ \text{V}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To find the electric potential at the surface of the conducting sphere, we first identify the known values from the question:
- Radius ($r$): $0.15\text{ m}$
- Charge ($Q$): $+6.0\ \mu\text{C}$

steps.title: Convert charge to Coulombs
steps.content:
Before substituting into our formula, we must ensure all units are in SI. The charge is given in microCoulombs ($\mu\text{C}$), so we convert it to Coulombs ($C$):

$$Q = 6.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$$

steps.title: Apply electric potential formula
steps.content:
For a conducting sphere, the electric potential at the surface is calculated using the formula:

$$V = \frac{kQ}{r}$$

where $k$ is Coulomb's constant, which we can find in the **Physical Constants** section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Substitute the values for $k$, $Q$, and $r$ into the equation:

$$V = \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.15}$$

Calculating this gives us the final potential.

steps.title: Use your calculator
steps.content: You can perform this calculation quickly using your graphing calculator. Since the result is $359,600$, we round it to three significant figures to match the precision of the constants provided.

gdc.instructions: Type the expression directly into the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen. Use the 'EE' key (2nd + ,) for scientific notation.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression using scientific notation.

calculator.description: Calculate the electric potential $V$.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The final value for the electric potential at the surface of the sphere is:

$$V = 3.60 \times 10^5\text{ V}$$

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
First, let's identify the values provided in the question and the constants we'll need from the IB Physics Data Booklet:

*   Radius of the sphere ($r$): $0.15\text{ m}$
*   Charge ($Q$): $+6.0\ \mu\text{C} = 6.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$
*   Coulomb constant ($k$): $8.99 \times 10^{9}\text{ N m}^{2}\text{C}^{-2}$

This question involves electric fields and potentials, which are covered in **Topic D.2: Electric and magnetic fields**.

steps.title: Calculate electric field strength
steps.content:
For a conducting sphere, the electric field strength $E$ at the surface can be calculated using the formula for a point charge. We first find the magnitude of this field using the formula from the data booklet:

$$E = \frac{kQ}{r^2}$$

Substituting our known values:

$$E = \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (6.0 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.15)^2}$$

$$E \approx 2.40 \times 10^6\text{ V m}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the result in the main calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula into the calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate the electric field strength E.

steps.title: Relate field strength to potential
steps.content:
For radial fields (like that of a point charge or a sphere), the electric potential $V$ is related to the field strength $E$ at a distance $r$ by the relationship:

$$V = E \times r$$

This is a specific case for point-charge distributions where potential is $V = \frac{kQ}{r}$ and field strength is $E = \frac{kQ}{r^2}$. Multiply your calculated field strength by the radius to find the potential at the surface.

steps.title: Calculate final potential
steps.content:
Now we perform the final calculation:

$$\begin{aligned} V &= (2,397,333.33) \times 0.15 \\ V &= 359,600\text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the precision used in the markscheme):

$$V = 3.60 \times 10^5\text{ V}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous answer by 0.15.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the result of the previous calculation by 0.15.

calculator.description: Multiply the field strength by the radius to get the potential.

parts.content: A charge of $+2.0\ \mu\text{C}$ is brought from infinity to the surface of the sphere. Calculate the work done by the electric field.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that the work done by the electric field is $W = -q\Delta V = -(2.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (3.60 \times 10^5)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final value for the work done: $W = -0.72\ \text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award 1 mark for the magnitude $0.72\ \text{J}$ if the negative sign is missing, as long as the method is correct.</note>

steps.title: Define the energy approach
steps.content:
To find the work done by the electric field, we can use the concept of electric potential energy ($E_p$). In a conservative field, the work done by the field is equal to the negative change in potential energy:

$$W_{\text{field}} = -\Delta E_p = -(E_{p, \text{final}} - E_{p, \text{initial}})$$

When a charge is at **infinity**, its electric potential energy is defined as zero ($E_{p, \text{initial}} = 0$).

steps.title: Calculate final potential energy
steps.content:
The final state is when the charge $q$ is at the surface of the sphere of radius $r$. The electric potential energy between two point charges (or a sphere and a point charge outside it) is given by:

$$E_p = \frac{kQq}{r}$$

Using the values provided:
- $k = 8.99 \times 10^9 \text{ N m}^2 \text{C}^{-2}$
- $Q = +6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C}$ (sphere charge)
- $q = +2.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C}$ (point charge)
- $r = 0.15 \text{ m}$

$$E_{p, \text{final}} = \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (6.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.15}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the product of k, Q, and q, then divide by the radius.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication and division as follows:

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the electric potential energy at the surface.

steps.title: Determine work done
steps.content:
From the calculation, we find $E_{p, \text{final}} \approx 0.72 \text{ J}$. Now, we find the work done by the field:

$$\begin{aligned} W_{\text{field}} &= -(E_{p, \text{final}} - E_{p, \text{initial}}) \\ W_{\text{field}} &= -(0.7192 \text{ J} - 0 \text{ J}) \\ W_{\text{field}} &\approx -0.72 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

The negative sign indicates that the electric field opposes the movement of the positive charge toward the positive sphere (work is done *against* the field).

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The work done by the electric field is $-0.72 \text{ J}$.

According to the markscheme, identifying the relationship between work and potential change earns the first mark (**M1**), and the correct final value with the sign earns the second mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To solve this problem using the **Electric Potential Approach**, we first list the given values and relevant constants from the data booklet:

- Radius of the sphere: $r = 0.15\text{ m}$
- Charge on the sphere: $Q = +6.0\ \mu\text{C} = 6.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$
- Charge being moved: $q = +2.0\ \mu\text{C} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$
- Coulomb constant: $k = 8.99 \times 10^9\ \text{N m}^2\ \text{C}^{-2}$

This problem involves concepts from **Topic D.2.4: Electric potential and work**.

steps.title: Calculate surface electric potential
steps.content:
The electric potential $V$ at the surface of a conducting sphere is calculated using the formula for a point charge, as the charge behaves as if it were concentrated at the center:

$$V = \frac{kQ}{r}$$

Substituting our values:

$$V_{\text{surface}} = \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (6.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.15}$$

steps.title: Verify potential calculation
steps.content: Using a calculator, we find the electric potential at the surface. At infinity, the potential $V_{\infty}$ is defined as $0\text{ V}$. Therefore, the change in potential is $\Delta V = V_{\text{surface}} - V_{\infty} = 3.60 \times 10^5\text{ V}$.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation directly: (8.99 * 10^9 * 6.0 * 10^-6) / 0.15

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the electric potential at the surface of the sphere.

steps.title: Calculate work done
steps.content:
The work done by the electric field ($W$) is defined as the negative change in electric potential energy:

$$W = -q\Delta V$$

Substituting the moving charge $q$ and the change in potential:

$$\begin{aligned} W &= -(2.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (3.596 \times 10^5) \\ W &= -0.7192\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

The negative sign indicates that the electric field is doing work *against* the motion (since both charges are positive and repel each other).

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to two significant figures (matching the precision of the input values), we get:

$$W = -0.72\ \text{J}$$

According to the markscheme, identifying the correct formula $W = -q\Delta V$ earns the first mark (**M1**), and the correct final value with units earns the second mark (**A1**).

parts.content: Explain the shape and spacing of equipotential surfaces around the sphere.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the equipotential surfaces are concentric spherical shells centered on the sphere. <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that the spacing between successive equipotential surfaces (of equal potential increments) increases with distance from the sphere because the field strength decreases (or field lines are radial and perpendicular to the surfaces). <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Determine the shape
steps.content:
For a charged conducting sphere, the electric field lines radiate outward symmetrically in all directions (radially). Equipotential surfaces are always perpendicular to the electric field lines. 

Since the field is perfectly symmetrical around the center of the sphere, the surfaces that remain perpendicular to these radial lines are **concentric spherical shells** centered on the sphere.

highlights.text: State that the equipotential surfaces are concentric spherical shells centered on the sphere.

steps.title: Analyze field strength
steps.content:
The electric field strength $E$ outside a charged sphere is given by the formula for a point charge:

$$E = k\frac{Q}{r^2}$$

This shows that as the distance $r$ from the center of the sphere increases, the electric field strength **decreases** (it follows an inverse-square law).

steps.title: Explain the spacing
steps.content:
The relationship between electric field strength and potential $V$ is defined by the potential gradient:

$$E = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}$$

where $\Delta r$ is the spacing between surfaces of equal potential increments ($\Delta V$). To maintain a constant $\Delta V$ between successive surfaces as the field strength $E$ decreases, the distance $\Delta r$ must **increase**. 

Therefore, the equipotential surfaces become further apart as you move away from the sphere.

highlights.text: Explain that the spacing between successive equipotential surfaces (of equal potential increments) increases with distance from the sphere because the field strength decreases

steps.title: Identify the Potential Formula
steps.content:
Outside a charged conducting sphere, the electric potential $V$ is treated as if all the charge $Q$ were concentrated at its center. This is described by the point charge potential formula found in **Topic D.2 (Electric and Magnetic Fields)**:

$$V = \frac{kQ}{r}$$

Where:
- $k$ is Coulomb's constant ($8.99 \times 10^9 \text{ N m}^2 \text{C}^{-2}$)
- $Q$ is the total charge
- $r$ is the distance from the center of the sphere

steps.title: Determine the Shape
steps.content:
An equipotential surface is a region where the potential $V$ is constant. From the formula $$V = \frac{kQ}{r}$$, for $V$ to remain the same, the distance $r$ from the center must also be constant.

Since the set of all points at a fixed distance from a central point forms a sphere, the equipotential surfaces are **concentric spherical shells** centered on the conducting sphere.

steps.title: Analyze the Spacing
steps.content:
To find how the spacing changes, we rearrange the formula for distance:

$$r = \frac{kQ}{V}$$

Because $r$ is inversely proportional to $V$, as the potential decreases (moving away from the sphere), equal steps in potential (e.g., drops of $50,000\text{ V}$) require increasingly larger changes in distance $r$.

This means the **spacing between successive equipotential surfaces increases** as you move further from the sphere.

ti84.instructions: Go to Y= and enter Y1 = (8.99*10^9 * 6.0*10^-6) / X. Use the Table (2nd + GRAPH) to see how X (distance) changes for specific Y (potential) values.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure you mention both characteristics:
1. The shape: **Concentric spherical shells**.
2. The spacing: **Increases with distance** from the sphere.

parts.content: Discuss the relationship between the gradient of electric potential and the electric field in this context.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the electric field strength is equal to the negative gradient of the electric potential: $E = -\frac{dV}{dr}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define the mathematical relationship
steps.content:
In the study of fields, the electric field strength $E$ is fundamentally defined by how the electric potential $V$ changes over a distance $r$. This relationship is expressed as the negative gradient of the potential:

$$E = -\frac{dV}{dr}$$

This means the magnitude of the electric field at any point is equal to the rate of change of the potential at that specific location.

steps.title: Explain the physical significance
steps.content:
The negative sign in the relationship $$E = -\frac{dV}{dr}$$ is crucial. It indicates that the electric field vector always points in the direction of the maximum decrease in electric potential. 

In the context of this positive $+6.0\ \mu\text{C}$ charge, the potential decreases as you move further away from the sphere ($r$ increases). Therefore, the electric field points radially outward, away from the region of higher potential.

steps.title: Summary for the markscheme
steps.content:
To earn the mark for this discussion, you must explicitly state that the electric field strength is equal to the negative gradient of the electric potential and provide the supporting equation.

This concept is a core part of **Topic D.2 (Electric and magnetic fields)**, specifically relating to how work and potential function in radial fields.

steps.title: Visualize the $V$ vs $r$ graph
steps.content:
To understand the relationship between potential ($V$) and the electric field ($E$), imagine a graph of $V$ against the distance from the center $r$:

*   **Inside the sphere ($r < 0.15\text{ m}$):** The potential is constant, so the graph is a flat, horizontal line.
*   **Outside the sphere ($r > 0.15\text{ m}$):** The potential decreases as distance increases ($V \propto \frac{1}{r}$), creating a downward-sloping curve.

steps.title: Analyze the graph's gradient
steps.content:
The **gradient** (or slope) of the $V$ vs $r$ graph at any point tells us how quickly the potential is changing with respect to distance. 

*   Inside the sphere, the slope is zero. 
*   Outside the sphere, the slope is negative because the potential decreases as we move further away. 

In physics, the magnitude of the electric field $E$ corresponds to the steepness of this slope.

steps.title: Define the relationship
steps.content:
The electric field strength $E$ is defined as the **negative gradient** of the electric potential $V$. Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$E = -\frac{dV}{dr}$$

This means that the electric field points in the direction of the maximum decrease in potential (from high potential to low potential).

highlights.text: electric field strength is equal to the negative gradient of the electric potential: $E = -\frac{dV}{dr}$

steps.title: Recall expressions for a sphere
steps.content:
To explore the relationship between potential and field, we first identify the expressions for the electric potential ($V$) and the electric field strength ($E$) outside a conducting sphere at a distance $r$ from its center:

$$\begin{aligned} V &= \frac{kQ}{r} \\ E &= \frac{kQ}{r^2} \end{aligned}$$

This falls under **Topic D.2: Electric and magnetic fields**.

steps.title: Calculate the potential gradient
steps.content:
The gradient of the electric potential is defined as the derivative of $V$ with respect to the distance $r$, written as $\frac{dV}{dr}$. 

We can rewrite the expression for potential as $V = kQ \cdot r^{-1}$ and apply the power rule for differentiation:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dV}{dr} &= \frac{d}{dr}(kQ \cdot r^{-1}) \\ \frac{dV}{dr} &= -1 \cdot kQ \cdot r^{-2} \\ \frac{dV}{dr} &= -\frac{kQ}{r^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Relate gradient to field
steps.content:
Now, compare our result for the gradient with the expression for the electric field strength:

1.  Potential gradient: $\frac{dV}{dr} = -\frac{kQ}{r^2}$
2.  Electric field: $E = \frac{kQ}{r^2}$

By substituting the second into the first, we see that:

$$\frac{dV}{dr} = -E$$

steps.title: State the final relationship
steps.content:
Rearranging the equation confirms the relationship required by the markscheme: the electric field strength is the negative gradient of the electric potential.

$$E = -\frac{dV}{dr}$$

This means the electric field points in the direction where the potential decreases most rapidly.

highlights.text: State that the electric field strength is equal to the negative gradient of the electric potential: E = -\frac{dV}{dr}

steps.title: Identify the potential formula
steps.content:
To find the electric potential at points A and B, we use the formula for the potential $V$ created by a point charge $Q$ at a distance $r$. This is a fundamental concept in **Topic D.2: Electric and magnetic fields**. 

The formula is:

$$V = \frac{kQ}{r}$$

where:
- $k$ is Coulomb's constant ($8.99 \times 10^{9} \text{ N m}^{2} \text{C}^{-2}$)
- $Q$ is the source charge ($+5.0\ \mu\text{C}$)
- $r$ is the distance from the charge

steps.title: Calculate potential at A
steps.content:
First, let's calculate the potential at point A. We are given the distance $r_A = 0.20\text{ m}$ and the charge $Q = +5.0\ \mu\text{C}$. 

Before substituting, we must convert the charge to Coulombs:
$$Q = 5.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$$

Now, substitute the values into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} V_A &= \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.20} \\ V_A &= 2.2475 \times 10^5\text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures to match the markscheme, we get $2.25 \times 10^5\text{ V}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter (8.99 × 10^9 × 5.0 × 10^-6) / 0.20 in the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation using scientific notation: (8.99E9 * 5.0E-6) / 0.20

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the calculator bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the electric potential at point A.

steps.title: Calculate potential at B
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the potential at point B, which is at a distance $r_B = 0.40\text{ m}$. Using the same charge $Q$:

$$\begin{aligned} V_B &= \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.40} \\ V_B &= 1.12375 \times 10^5\text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures as required by the markscheme, we get $1.12 \times 10^5\text{ V}$.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation: (8.99E9 * 5.0E-6) / 0.40

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the calculator bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the electric potential at point B.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
The electric potentials are:
- At point A: $V_A = 2.25 \times 10^5\text{ V}$
- At point B: $V_B = 1.12 \times 10^5\text{ V}$

In an exam, you earn one mark for each correct potential calculation including units.

steps.title: Identify potential formula
steps.content:
To calculate the electric potential ($V$) produced by a point charge ($Q$) at a distance ($r$), we use the formula:

$$V = \frac{kQ}{r}$$

This formula is a fundamental concept in **Topic D.2: Electric and magnetic fields**. Here, $k$ is Coulomb's constant, which we can find in the Physical Constants section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Calculate potential at point A
steps.content:
We substitute the values for the source charge and the distance to point A into the potential formula:

- Source charge $Q = +5.0\ \mu\text{C} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$
- Distance $r_A = 0.20\ \text{m}$

$$\begin{aligned} V_A &= \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.20} \\ V_A &= 2.2475 \times 10^5\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $V_A = 2.25 \times 10^5\ \text{V}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the quotient of (k * Q) and r_A.

ti84.instructions: Enter the product of k and Q, then divide by the radius r_A.

desmos.instructions: Enter the formula directly using scientific notation.

calculator.description: Calculate the electric potential at point A.

steps.title: Establish the distance ratio
steps.content:
Now we compare the distance of point B to the distance of point A. 

$$\frac{r_B}{r_A} = \frac{0.40\ \text{m}}{0.20\ \text{m}} = 2$$

Point B is exactly twice as far from the origin as point A.

steps.title: Use Ratio Reasoning for B
steps.content:
Since $V = \frac{kQ}{r}$, we know that potential is inversely proportional to distance ($V \propto \frac{1}{r}$).

Because the distance has doubled ($r_B = 2r_A$), the potential must be halved:

$$\begin{aligned} V_B &= V_A \times \frac{1}{2} \\ V_B &= 2.2475 \times 10^5 \times 0.5 \\ V_B &= 1.12375 \times 10^5\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $V_B = 1.12 \times 10^5\ \text{V}$.

gdc.instructions: Divide the value of V_A by 2.

ti84.instructions: Divide the previous answer for V_A by 2.

desmos.instructions: Calculate half of the potential at A.

calculator.description: Calculate the electric potential at point B by halving the potential at A.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
The electric potentials are:
- $V_A = 2.25 \times 10^5\ \text{V}$
- $V_B = 1.12 \times 10^5\ \text{V}$

In the markscheme, 1 mark is awarded for each correct potential calculation.

parts.content: Determine the work done by the electric field in moving the test charge from A to B.
parts.markscheme:
- Use formula for work done by field: $W_{\text{field}} = -q\Delta V = -q(V_B - V_A)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute and calculate: $W = -1.0 \times 10^{-6} (1.12 \times 10^5 - 2.25 \times 10^5) = +0.113\ \text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
To find the work done by the electric field, we use the relationship between work, charge, and the change in electric potential. The work done by an internal field is equal to the negative change in potential energy:

$$W_{\text{field}} = -q\Delta V = -q(V_B - V_A)$$

This concept is part of **Topic D.2: Electric and magnetic fields**. Here, $q$ is the test charge, and $V_A$ and $V_B$ are the electric potentials at the starting and ending points, respectively.

steps.title: Calculate potential at A
steps.content:
We first calculate the electric potential $V_A$ created by the source charge ($Q = +5.0\ \mu\text{C}$) at point A ($r = 0.20\ \text{m}$). Using the formula for point charge potential:

$$V = \frac{kQ}{r}$$

Substituting the values:

$$V_A = \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.20} = 2.2475 \times 10^5\ \text{V}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the expression for the potential at point A.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula for V_A.

calculator.description: Calculate electric potential at A.

steps.title: Calculate potential at B
steps.content:
Now, we calculate the potential $V_B$ at point B ($r = 0.40\ \text{m}$):

$$V_B = \frac{8.99 \times 10^9 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.40} = 1.12375 \times 10^5\ \text{V}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the expression for the potential at point B.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula for V_B.

calculator.description: Calculate electric potential at B.

steps.title: Calculate work done
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the work done by the field using the test charge $q = +1.0\ \mu\text{C}$:

$$\begin{aligned} W_{\text{field}} &= -q(V_B - V_A) \\ W_{\text{field}} &= -(1.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1.12375 \times 10^5 - 2.2475 \times 10^5) \\ W_{\text{field}} &= -(1.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (-1.12375 \times 10^5) \\ W_{\text{field}} &= +0.112375\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (as per the input values), we get $+0.11\ \text{J}$ (or $0.113\ \text{J}$ using 3 sig figs as often seen in markschemes).

gdc.instructions: Subtract the potentials and multiply by -q.

ti84.instructions: Use the previously calculated values for VA and VB.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the work done by the field.

calculator.description: Calculate final work done.

steps.title: Identify the approach
steps.content:
We are asked to find the work done by the electric field ($W_{\text{field}}$). When a charge moves in an electric field, the field does work as the system's potential energy changes. The relationship is:
$$W_{\text{field}} = E_{p,\text{initial}} - E_{p,\text{final}}$$

This concept is closely related to the conservation of energy discussed in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Calculate potential energy at A
steps.content:
First, let's find the electric potential energy ($E_p$) at point A, which is $0.20\ \text{m}$ from the source charge. We use the formula for electric potential energy between two point charges:
$$E_p = \frac{kQq}{r}$$

Substituting the values:
- $k = 8.99 \times 10^9\ \text{N m}^2 \text{C}^{-2}$
- $Q = +5.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$
- $q = +1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$
- $r_A = 0.20\ \text{m}$

$$E_{p,A} = \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9)(5.0 \times 10^{-6})(1.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.20} = 0.22475\ \text{J}$$

**Rounding to 3 significant figures:** $E_{p,A} \approx 0.225\ \text{J}$

steps.title: Calculate potential energy at B
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the potential energy at point B, which is $0.40\ \text{m}$ from the origin:
$$E_{p,B} = \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9)(5.0 \times 10^{-6})(1.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.40} = 0.112375\ \text{J}$$

**Rounding to 3 significant figures:** $E_{p,B} \approx 0.112\ \text{J}$

steps.title: Determine work done
steps.content:
The work done by the electric field is the difference between the initial and final potential energy:
$$W_{\text{field}} = E_{p,A} - E_{p,B}$$
$$W_{\text{field}} = 0.22475 - 0.112375 = 0.112375\ \text{J}$$

Following the precision used in the markscheme (which rounds intermediate potential values to 3 significant figures before subtracting):
$$W_{\text{field}} = 0.225\ \text{J} - 0.112\ \text{J} = 0.113\ \text{J}$$

Since the work is positive, it means the electric field is "pushing" the charge in the direction it is moving (repelling it).

steps.content: You can use your GDC to perform these calculations quickly in one go to avoid rounding errors until the end.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, let's list the known values from the question and the data booklet. Note that we must convert microcoulombs ($\mu\text{C}$) to coulombs ($\text{C}$) for our calculations:

*   Source charge ($Q$) = $+5.0\ \mu\text{C} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$
*   Test charge ($q$) = $+1.0\ \mu\text{C} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$
*   Initial distance ($r_A$) = $0.20\ \text{m}$
*   Final distance ($r_B$) = $0.40\ \text{m}$
*   Coulomb constant ($k$) = $8.99 \times 10^9\ \text{N m}^2\ \text{C}^{-2}$

steps.title: Calculate initial potential energy
steps.content:
To find the potential energy at point A ($E_{p,A}$), we use the formula for the electric potential energy of a point charge system:

$$E_p = \frac{kQq}{r}$$

Substituting the values for point A:

$$E_{p,A} = \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.20}$$

$$E_{p,A} = 0.22475\ \text{J}$$

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate the initial potential energy at point A.

steps.title: Calculate final potential energy
steps.content:
Now, we calculate the potential energy at point B ($E_{p,B}$) by using the final distance $r_B = 0.40\ \text{m}$:

$$E_{p,B} = \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.40}$$

$$E_{p,B} = 0.112375\ \text{J}$$

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate the final potential energy at point B.

steps.title: Calculate energy change
steps.content:
The change in potential energy ($\Delta E_p$) is the final energy minus the initial energy:

$$\Delta E_p = E_{p,B} - E_{p,A}$$

$$\Delta E_p = 0.112375 - 0.22475 = -0.112375\ \text{J}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:

$$\Delta E_p = -0.113\ \text{J}$$

desmos.instructions: Enter the subtraction directly.

calculator.description: Find the difference between the final and initial potential energy.

steps.title: Interpret the result
steps.content:
The negative sign indicates that the potential energy decreased. This makes physical sense because both charges are positive (they repel each other). Moving the test charge further away from the source charge allows the field to do work, resulting in a loss of stored potential energy.

Final Answer:
$$\Delta E_p = -0.113\ \text{J}$$

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, we identify the key values from the question and the data booklet constants:
- Source charge $Q = +5.0\ \mu\text{C} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$
- Test charge $q = +1.0\ \mu\text{C} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$
- Initial distance $r_A = 0.20\ \text{m}$
- Final distance $r_B = 0.40\ \text{m}$
- Coulomb constant $k = 8.99 \times 10^9\ \text{N m}^2 \text{C}^{-2}$

steps.title: Calculate potential at A
steps.content:
We calculate the electric potential $V$ at distance $r$ using the formula $V = \frac{kQ}{r}$. 

For point A at $r_A = 0.20\ \text{m}$:

$$\begin{aligned} V_A &= \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.20} \\ V_A &= 224,750\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculation of potential at point A

steps.title: Calculate potential at B
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the electric potential at point B ($r_B = 0.40\ \text{m}$):

$$\begin{aligned} V_B &= \frac{(8.99 \times 10^9) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-6})}{0.40} \\ V_B &= 112,375\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculation of potential at point B

steps.title: Find potential difference
steps.content:
The potential difference $\Delta V$ between point A and point B is given by:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta V &= V_B - V_A \\ \Delta V &= 112,375 - 224,750 \\ \Delta V &= -112,375\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate potential energy change
steps.content:
The change in electric potential energy $\Delta E_p$ is found by multiplying the test charge $q$ by the potential difference $\Delta V$:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta E_p &= q \times \Delta V \\ \Delta E_p &= (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (-112,375) \\ \Delta E_p &= -0.112375\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to significant figures, we get approximately **$-0.11\ \text{J}$** (or $-0.113\ \text{J}$ as noted in the markscheme).

calculator.description: Calculate the final energy change.

parts.content: Use the electric potential gradient to estimate the average electric field strength between points A and B.
parts.markscheme:
- Use potential gradient: $E = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r} = -\frac{1.12 \times 10^5 - 2.25 \times 10^5}{0.20}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate average field strength: $E = 5.65 \times 10^5\ \text{V m}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the Method
steps.content:
To estimate the average electric field strength between two points, we can use the relationship between electric field ($E$) and the electric potential gradient. In a radial field, the average electric field strength is approximately equal to the negative change in potential divided by the change in distance.

$$E = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}$$

steps.title: Calculate Potential at A
steps.content:
First, we calculate the electric potential ($V_A$) at point A ($r = 0.20\text{ m}$) due to the $+5.0\ \mu\text{C}$ charge at the origin. Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$V = k\frac{Q}{r}$$

Substituting the values:
$$V_A = (8.99 \times 10^9) \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.20} \approx 2.25 \times 10^5\text{ V}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the electric potential at point A.

steps.title: Calculate Potential at B
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the electric potential ($V_B$) at point B ($r = 0.40\text{ m}$):

$$V_B = (8.99 \times 10^9) \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.40} \approx 1.12 \times 10^5\text{ V}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the electric potential at point B.

steps.title: Calculate Average Field Strength
steps.content:
Now, we find the potential gradient between A and B. The distance between the points is $\Delta r = 0.40 - 0.20 = 0.20\text{ m}$.

Using the potential gradient formula:
$$E = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta r} = -\frac{V_B - V_A}{\Delta r}$$

$$E = -\frac{1.12 \times 10^5 - 2.25 \times 10^5}{0.20}$$

$$E \approx 5.65 \times 10^5\text{ V m}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the average electric field strength using the potential difference.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The estimated average electric field strength between points A and B is $5.65 \times 10^5\text{ V m}^{-1}$.

In the exam, one mark is awarded for correctly applying the potential gradient formula and one mark for the final numerical result.

steps.title: Calculate potential energy change
steps.content:
To find the average electric field, we first determine the change in electric potential energy ($\Delta E_p$) as the test charge moves from point A to point B. 

Using the values for electric potential ($V$) calculated previously (or using $V = \frac{kQ}{r}$):
- Potential at A ($r = 0.20\text{ m}$): $V_A = 2.25 \times 10^5\text{ V}$
- Potential at B ($r = 0.40\text{ m}$): $V_B = 1.12 \times 10^5\text{ V}$

The change in potential energy is given by:
$$\Delta E_p = q \Delta V = q(V_B - V_A)$$

$$\Delta E_p = (1.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}) \times (1.12 \times 10^5\text{ V} - 2.25 \times 10^5\text{ V}) = -0.113\text{ J}$$

The magnitude of the work done by the field is $0.113\text{ J}$.

steps.title: Find the average force
steps.content:
Work done ($W$) is related to the average force ($F$) and the distance moved ($\Delta r$) along the direction of the force. Using the formula from the data booklet:

$$W = Fs\cos\theta$$

Since the movement is radial, $\cos\theta = 1$. We can find the average force by equating the work done to the change in potential energy:

$$F_{avg} = \frac{W}{\Delta r} = \frac{0.113\text{ J}}{0.40\text{ m} - 0.20\text{ m}}$$

$$F_{avg} = \frac{0.113}{0.20} = 0.565\text{ N}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.113 / 0.20 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 0.113 / 0.20 in the expression field.

calculator.description: Divide the work done by the distance to find the average force.

steps.title: Determine average field strength
steps.content:
Finally, we determine the average electric field strength ($E$) using the relationship between force and field from the data booklet:

$$E = \frac{F}{q}$$

Substitute the average force and the test charge ($q = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$):

$$E = \frac{0.565\text{ N}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}} = 5.65 \times 10^5\text{ V m}^{-1}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.565 / 1E-6 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 0.565 / 10^{-6} in the expression field.

calculator.description: Calculate the field strength by dividing the force by the charge.

parts.content: Describe the relationship between electric field lines and equipotential surfaces in this configuration.
parts.markscheme:
- State relationship: Equipotential surfaces are perpendicular to electric field lines. In radial fields, field lines are radial, and equipotentials are concentric spheres. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: State the general rule
steps.content: The fundamental geometric relationship in any electric field is that **electric field lines are always perpendicular to equipotential surfaces**. This means that at any point in space, the direction of the field and the surface of constant potential meet at a $90^\circ$ angle.

steps.title: Apply to point charge
steps.content: For the specific configuration of a **$+5.0\ \mu\text{C}$ point charge**, the electric field lines are **radial**, spreading out in all directions from the origin. To maintain the perpendicular relationship with these radial lines, the equipotential surfaces must be **concentric spheres** centered at the origin.

steps.title: Final descriptive summary
steps.content:
In summary, the field lines radiate outward like the spokes of a wheel, while the equipotentials form nested shells (spheres) around the charge. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic D.2 (Electric and magnetic fields)**. To earn the mark, you must mention both the perpendicular nature and the specific shapes (radial lines and spherical surfaces).

steps.title: Define equipotential surfaces
steps.content:
An **equipotential surface** is a collection of points in space where the electric potential ($V$) is exactly the same. Because the potential is constant, if we move a test charge along this surface, the change in potential ($\Delta V$) is zero. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic D.2 (Electric and magnetic fields)**.

steps.title: Analyze work and energy
steps.content:
By definition, the work done ($W$) to move a charge ($q$) is related to the change in potential energy, which is given by $W = q\Delta V$. 

Since $\Delta V = 0$ when moving along an equipotential surface, the total work done by the electric field on the charge is **zero** ($W = 0$).

steps.title: Apply work-force relationship
steps.content:
From **Topic A.3 (Work, energy and power)**, we know the formula for work done by a force ($F$) over a displacement ($s$):

$$\text{Work} = Fs \cos \theta$$

In this case, since work is zero ($W = 0$) and the force and displacement are not zero, the term $\cos \theta$ must be zero. This only happens when the angle $\theta$ between the force and the direction of motion is $90^\circ$.

gdc.instructions: Type 'cos(90)' into the calculation bar. Ensure the angle unit is set to degrees.

ti84.instructions: Press the [COS] key, type 90, and press [ENTER]. Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode.

desmos.instructions: Type 'cos(90)' and ensure the toggle is set to 'Degrees'.

calculator.description: Verify that the cosine of 90 degrees results in zero work.

steps.title: Connect force to field
steps.content:
The electric force $F$ acts in the same direction as the electric field $E$ for a positive charge, as shown by the relationship:

$$E = \frac{F}{q}$$

Since the force must be perpendicular to the direction of motion along the surface to ensure zero work is done, the **electric field lines must always be perpendicular to equipotential surfaces**.

steps.title: State specific configuration
steps.content:
For a point charge at the origin:
1. The **electric field lines** spread out radially in all directions.
2. To remain perpendicular to these radial lines, the **equipotential surfaces** must be **concentric spheres** centered on the charge.

questions.specification:
The diagram shows a particle of mass $m$ and charge $-q$ in uniform circular motion around a fixed point charge $+q$. The particle moves at constant speed $v$ in a circular orbit of radius $r$ due to the electrostatic force of attraction between the charges.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/Ff4JAss4-qmvdthnRg0PW.jpeg)
